title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Your Relationships
meta_description: Trust the quiet whisper your mind tries to override. It's your intuition, not anxiety. Learn to evaluate relationships through your gut feelings, not overthinking.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Your Relationships
og_description: Trust the quiet whisper your mind tries to override. It's your intuition, not anxiety. Learn to evaluate relationships through your gut feelings, not overthinking.
body:
Your Gut Is Already Talking to You About Them - You Just Keep Ignoring It

You know that feeling. The one that hits you in the chest when you're sitting across from someone you care about, and something just feels... off. It's not loud. It's not dramatic. It's a quiet whisper that says "this isn't right" or "pay attention here." And almost immediately, your brain jumps in to explain it away. I'm being too sensitive. They didn't mean it that way. I'm overthinking again.

But here's the thing: that whisper isn't your anxiety. It's not your trauma. It's your intuition - and it's been trying to get your attention for a long time. You just haven't learned to trust it yet.

I'm Kalesh, and I've spent years working with people who feel everything deeply. People who walk into a room and immediately know who's safe and who isn't. People who get a sinking feeling in their stomach when something's wrong in a relationship, even when everything looks fine on paper. Sound familiar? Right?!

You're not broken. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range of input than most people's. And when it comes to relationships - romantic, platonic, family, work - your intuition is one of the most reliable tools you have. You just need to learn how to use it without second-guessing yourself into oblivion.

What Intuition Actually Is (And Isn't)

Let's clear something up right now. Intuition isn't magic. It's not psychic powers or some woo-woo thing reserved for people who meditate on mountaintops. Intuition is your brain's ability to process information faster than your conscious mind can keep up with. It's pattern recognition. It's your body keeping score of every interaction you've ever had, every micro-expression you've ever seen, every time someone's words didn't match their energy.

Your intuition is a data-processing system that runs in the background 24/7. And when it flags something in a relationship, it's because it's picked up on a mismatch - between what someone says and what they do, between how they make you feel and how they should make you feel, between the story you're telling yourself and the truth you already know.

"Your intuition isn't a random feeling - it's your whole history of experience talking to you in shorthand. When you learn to listen, you stop wasting years on people who were never right for you."

The problem is that most of us were trained out of trusting this system. We were told to be polite, to give people the benefit of the doubt, to not make snap judgments. And sure, there's wisdom in not being rash. But there's also wisdom in not ignoring the massive red flag your gut is waving in your face.

Why Your Brain Lies to You About Relationships

Here's where it gets tricky. Your intuition is almost never wrong. But your brain - your conscious, logical, storytelling brain - is really good at talking you out of what you already know. It does this for a few reasons.

First, your brain hates uncertainty. When you've invested time, energy, and emotion into a relationship, your brain wants that investment to pay off. So it will manufacture reasons to stay. It will rationalize bad behavior. It will reframe red flags as orange flags, then yellow flags, then "maybe I'm just being dramatic" flags.

Second, your brain is addicted to familiar patterns. If you grew up in an environment where love felt conditional, chaotic, or inconsistent, your brain learned that this is what love looks like. So when you meet someone who feels familiar - even if that familiarity is painful - your brain will tell you it's fine. It's what you know. It's comfortable, even when it hurts.

Third, your brain is terrified of being wrong. Admitting that a relationship isn't working means admitting you made a mistake. And most of us would rather stay in a bad situation than face the shame of having chosen poorly. So your brain will keep spinning stories to keep you stuck.

Does that land? Because I want you to really hear this: your brain is not your enemy. It's trying to protect you. But it's protecting you from the wrong things. It's protecting you from short-term discomfort at the cost of long-term peace. And your intuition knows the difference.

How to Tell the Difference Between Intuition and Fear

This is the question I get more than any other. "How do I know if it's my intuition or my anxiety?" And it's a fair question, because both can feel similar. Both can show up as a tight chest, a restless feeling, a sense that something is wrong. But there are key differences.

Intuition is calm. Even when it's telling you something hard, it feels grounded. It's a knowing, not a spiraling. It doesn't come with a list of worst-case scenarios. It just says "this isn't right" and then gets quiet.

Fear is loud. Fear is the voice that says "what if they leave, what if I'm alone forever, what if I'm making a huge mistake, what if no one else will ever love me." Fear is a runaway train of catastrophic thinking. It keeps you up at 3 AM with a racing heart and a spinning mind.

Intuition is a single, clear note. Fear is a symphony of chaos.

So when you're trying to evaluate a relationship, start by checking your nervous system. Take a breath. Put your hand on your chest. Ask yourself: what do I actually know, without the story I'm telling myself about it?

If the answer is something simple - "they don't make me feel safe" or "I'm always walking on eggshells" or "something about the way they talk about their ex bothers me" - that's intuition. It's direct. It doesn't need a five-paragraph explanation.

If the answer is a tangled mess of hypotheticals and worst-case scenarios - that's fear. And fear needs to be soothed, not followed.

One book that really helped me understand this distinction is The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker. It's not a psychic development book - it's a book about safety and survival instincts. But the way he breaks down how your intuition speaks to you before your brain catches up is pure gold. See on Amazon (paid link)

Practical Steps to Use Intuition in Your Relationships

Okay, so you're ready to start actually using this thing. Here's how you do it - step by step, no fluff, no spiritual bypassing.

1. Create Space to Hear Yourself

You can't hear your intuition if you're constantly drowning in noise. Noise from your phone, noise from other people's opinions, noise from your own endless internal chatter. You need quiet. Not necessarily silence - but quiet. A walk without headphones. A few minutes in the morning before you check your phone. A moment in the shower where you're not planning your day.

In that quiet, ask yourself one question about the relationship you're evaluating: "How do I feel when I'm with this person?" Not what you think about them. Not what you hope they'll become. How do you feel - in your body, in your gut, in your bones?

If the answer is "tense" or "small" or "like I have to perform" - that's data. Don't argue with it. Just notice it.

2. Stop Explaining Away Red Flags

This is the hard one. When your intuition flags something, your brain will immediately start generating explanations. "They were just tired." "They've been under a lot of stress." "They didn't mean it that way." "I'm too sensitive."

Here's a rule I want you to try: when you feel something off, don't explain it for 24 hours. Just sit with the feeling. Don't act on it. Don't confront anyone. Don't decide anything. Just let the feeling exist without your brain trying to fix it.

What you'll find is that most of those explanations are just stories you're telling yourself to avoid discomfort. The feeling itself - the actual intuitive hit - doesn't change. It just sits there, waiting for you to stop running from it.

3. Ask Your Body Direct Questions

Your body is smarter than your brain when it comes to relationships. Your brain can be fooled by charm, by promises, by hope. Your body can't. Your body knows who's safe and who isn't, because it's been tracking safety since you were born.

So ask your body direct questions. Close your eyes. Bring the person to mind. Then ask: "Does my body relax or tighten when I think about them?" "Do I feel expansive or contracted?" "Do I want to move toward them or away?"

Don't judge the answer. Just notice it. If your body tightens, that's a no. If it relaxes, that's a yes. It's that simple.

And if you're someone who's been disconnected from your body for a while - which is common for people who've been through trauma or who were taught that feelings are unreliable - you might need to rebuild that connection. Becoming Supernatural by Dr. Joe Dispenza has some excellent practices for getting back in touch with your body's wisdom. See on Amazon (paid link)

4. Look for Patterns, Not Isolated Incidents

One red flag is a data point. Three red flags in the same pattern is a message. Your intuition is really good at spotting patterns, even when you're not consciously looking for them.

So when you're evaluating a relationship, don't focus on that one fight or that one comment. Look at the overall shape of the relationship. How do you feel most of the time? Do you feel safe, seen, and respected? Or do you feel anxious, confused, and small?

Your intuition is already tracking this. It knows the percentages. It knows that a relationship where you feel good 30% of the time and bad 70% of the time is not a good relationship, no matter how good that 30% feels.

Trust the trend, not the exception.

5. Practice on Small Decisions First

If you're not used to trusting your intuition, don't start with "should I break up with my partner?" Start small. Start with "should I go to this event or stay home?" or "should I respond to this text now or wait?"

Every time you make a small decision based on intuition and it works out, you're building trust with yourself. You're teaching your brain that this system is reliable. And when the big decisions come - the relationship crossroads - you'll have a track record of listening and being right.

This is a skill. You have to practice it.

6. Get Out of Your Head and Into Your Environment

One of the fastest ways to access intuition is to change your environment. Go for a walk in nature. Sit by water. Move your body. When you're stuck in your head, you can't hear anything but your own thoughts. When you're in your body, in the physical world, your intuition has room to speak.

I've had clients who couldn't figure out a relationship dynamic for months, and then they went for a hike and the answer hit them in five minutes. That's not magic. That's just getting out of the way.

There's a beautiful book called Braiding Sweetgrass by Robin Wall Kimmerer that talks about this kind of deep listening - not just to people, but to the world around you. It's not a relationship book per se, but the way she writes about connection and reciprocity will shift how you think about every relationship in your life. See on Amazon (paid link)

What to Do When Your Intuition Says "No"

This is where the rubber meets the road. You've listened. You've asked your body. You've sat with the feeling. And your intuition is clear: this relationship isn't right. Now what?

First, don't panic. Intuition gives you information, not a deadline. You don't have to act immediately. You can sit with the knowing for a while. You can gather your resources. You can plan your exit or your boundary conversation or whatever needs to happen next.

Second, stop trying to change the person. If your intuition is telling you that someone isn't capable of giving you what you need, no amount of love, patience, or "working on it" is going to change that. People change when they want to change, not when you want them to.

Third, trust that your intuition is on your side. It's not trying to ruin your life. It's not trying to make you lonely. It's trying to save you from wasting years on something that was never going to work. It's trying to clear space for the relationships that actually nourish you.

And that's the thing nobody tells you about intuition: it's not just about avoiding bad relationships. It's about making room for good ones. Every time you say no to something that isn't right, you're saying yes to something that could be. You just can't see it yet.

The Bright Wound of Being Too Sensitive

You know what I'm talking about. The wound that comes from feeling too much, caring too deeply, sensing things other people don't seem to notice. You've probably been told your whole life that you're too much. Too emotional. Too sensitive. Too intense.

But here's the reframe: that sensitivity is not a weakness. It's a finely tuned instrument. It's the reason you can walk into a room and know who's genuine. It's the reason you can sense when something's wrong in a relationship before the fight even happens. It's the reason you're reading this article instead of ignoring the quiet voice that's been trying to get your attention.

The wound is real. It hurts to feel things so deeply in a world that tells you not to. But the brightness - the clarity, the insight, the ability to see through people's masks - that's the gift. And when you learn to trust it, you stop getting blindsided by relationships that drain you. You stop ignoring your own knowing. You start choosing people who actually see you, instead of people who just make you feel less alone.

A Simple Check-In You Can Use Right Now

If you're in the middle of evaluating a relationship and you're not sure what your intuition is saying, try this. Right now, wherever you are, take three deep breaths. Close your eyes if you can. Bring the person to mind. Then ask yourself these questions:

  • Do I feel safe being fully myself with this person?
  • Do I feel like I have to earn their love or approval?
  • Do I feel more energized or more drained after spending time with them?
  • Do I trust that they have my best interests at heart?
  • If a friend described this relationship to me, what would I tell them?

Don't overthink your answers. Don't analyze them. Just let them come. Your intuition already knows the answers. You just have to be willing to hear them.

And if you're scared of what you'll hear - that's normal. That's human. That's the part of you that's afraid of being alone, of starting over, of being wrong. But here's the truth: staying in a relationship that isn't right is its own kind of loneliness. It's a loneliness you can't fix by trying harder or being more patient or loving them more. It's a loneliness that only goes away when you honor what you already know.

You Already Know. You've Always Known.

I'm going to say something that might sting, but I need you to hear it: you already know what your intuition is telling you about this relationship. You've known for a while. You've just been hoping it would change. Hoping they would change. Hoping you were wrong.

You weren't wrong. You were just scared. And that's okay. Fear is not a failure. It's a signal. It's information. And now that you know the difference between fear and intuition, you can start making decisions from a place of clarity instead of panic.

Your relationships are the mirror of your inner world. When you learn to trust your intuition, you stop attracting people who confuse you, drain you, or make you question your own reality. You start attracting people who feel like home. People who don't require you to shrink or perform or pretend. People who see your bright wound and don't run from it - they sit with it, and they let you sit with theirs.

That's what's waiting for you on the other side of trusting yourself. And it's closer than you think.

If you're ready to go deeper into this work - to really untangle the patterns that keep you stuck in relationships that don't serve you - I've put together a short assessment that can help you see exactly where your intuition is already trying to guide you. It takes about five minutes, and it might show you something you've been avoiding. Take the assessment here.
